nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered reef walls, overhangs, and lagoons; tends to favor turbid, low-flow zones similar to standard bubble coral, often found on shaded or partially shaded reef surfaces

decorPreferences: lower rockwork or sand bed placement
flat stable surface to prevent toppling
open clearance from neighboring corals (15+ cm)
shaded or partially shaded position acceptable

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae, which can supply a large portion of energy needs under adequate moderate lighting. Target feeding once per week with meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, or finely chopped seafood is generally considered beneficial. Food should be gently directed onto the expanded vesicles using a turkey baster or pipette with flow reduced or off for several minutes. Has been known to regurgitate if overfed.

goodWith: peaceful reef fish (gobies, blennies, small wrasses)
other LPS corals with adequate spacing (15+ cm clearance)
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle reach (10–15 cm radius)
high-flow species that require strong or direct currents
species that may nip or pick at fleshy polyps

summary: Grape Cristata Coral (Plerogyra sp.) is a smaller-bubbled variant of the bubble coral complex with distinctively compact or lobate vesicles. Intermediate LPS; prefers low flow and low-moderate light; long sweeper tentacles require spacing.
careNotes:
Grape Cristata Coral is a trade name applied to Plerogyra sp. specimens that display smaller, more compact, or differently shaped vesicles than the standard Bubble Coral (Plerogyra sinuosa). The exact species identity is not always resolved at point of sale; specimens sold under this name are most often considered to be Plerogyra simplex (the Branching Bubble Coral, characterized by tubular branching growth and pillow-shaped vesicles at branch tips) or occasionally Plerogyra multilobata (with shaft-shaped vesicles bearing 3–7 bulbs along the upper edge). Both are distinct from P. sinuosa, which features larger smooth grape-like vesicles and a meandroid skeleton.

Husbandry requirements for Grape Cristata are broadly shared across the Plerogyra genus and closely mirror those for standard Bubble Coral:

• Flow: Low to low-moderate indirect flow is generally preferred. Strong or direct flow has been known to prevent full vesicle expansion and may damage the fragile tissue. Random, non-laminar flow is preferred.
• Lighting: Low to moderate intensity, generally considered suitable in the range of 50–120 PAR. Placement on lower rockwork or the sand bed is commonly recommended. Gradual light acclimation is advised when transitioning from lower to higher intensity.
• Placement: Lower third of the aquarium, on a flat and stable surface. The coral cannot self-right if toppled; securing with reef-safe epoxy is advisable.
• Aggression: Long sweeper tentacles have been reported to extend 10–15 cm or more at night. These can sting and damage neighboring corals. Maintaining at least 15 cm of clearance from other specimens is strongly advised.
• Vesicle fragility: The fleshy vesicles puncture easily. Handle only by the hard skeleton. Brown jelly infection (a bacterial condition) has been reported to occur following physical trauma to the tissue.
• Feeding: Weekly target feeding with meaty foods is generally considered beneficial. Zooxanthellae provide a substantial fraction of nutritional needs, but supplemental feeding tends to support better coloration and growth rate.

Distinction from standard Bubble Coral (Plerogyra sinuosa): Both are members of the genus Plerogyra and share nearly identical husbandry requirements. The Grape Cristata trade form is distinguished by notably smaller or more distinctly shaped vesicles — sometimes lobate, multi-lobed, or arranged on branching stalks depending on the exact species — compared to the large, smooth, uniform grape-like vesicles of P. sinuosa. In the aquarium trade, buyers should request a clear species identification when possible; P. simplex tends to form branching colonies, while P. sinuosa forms a compact meandroid structure. All Plerogyra species are considered challenging to distinguish without examining the skeleton under deflated polyps or with expert knowledge.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs. Captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby. Fragging is generally considered impractical across the Plerogyra genus due to the soft tissue arrangement and the difficulty of cutting through the skeleton without injuring the polyp.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Bubble Coral (Plerogyra sinuosa)
primary.notes: Primary data for Plerogyra genus-level husbandry (water parameters, flow, lighting, feeding, temperament). No dedicated Grape Cristata page found in current LiveAquaria inventory. Plerogyra sp. care parameters are genus-consistent with P. sinuosa. LiveAquaria family listed as Euphylliidae; Wikipedia lists Plerogyridae — Euphylliidae used here per aquarium-source consensus.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Plerogyra sinuosa
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Order: Scleractinia, Family: Plerogyridae/Euphylliidae — see notes), native range (Red Sea and Madagascar to Okinawa and Line Islands), habitat (low light, gentle flow), and colony size. Wikipedia lists family as Plerogyridae; aquarium hobby sources predominantly use Euphylliidae.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Bubble Coral: How to Identify Four Species
additional.notes: Key secondary source for species-level distinction within Plerogyra. Confirmed P. simplex (Branching Bubble: tubular branches, pillow-shaped vesicles) and P. sinuosa (standard bubble: smooth grape-like vesicles, meandroid skeleton) as distinct species. Habitat notes: protected environments with minimal flow, walls and overhangs, often turbid water. Confirmed feeding tentacle emergence at night.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Plerogyra diabolotus, the Devil's Bubble Coral
additional.notes: Additional context on Plerogyra species diversity. Confirmed P. multilobata as a distinct species with shaft-shaped multi-lobed vesicles, which may be encountered under the Grape Cristata trade name. Species was described in 2003 and imported from Sulawesi, Indonesia.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Plerogyra (genus overview)
additional.notes: Confirmed genus taxonomy (Family: Plerogyridae, Order: Scleractinia), geographic range (Indian and Pacific Oceans, Red Sea). Listed species within genus: P. sinuosa, P. simplex, P. multilobata, P. diabolotus, P. cauliformis, P. discus, P. eurysepta.
